fragments.content: Hyperkalemia is serum potassium >5.0-5.5 mEq/L, a potentially life-threatening electrolyte abnormality. Causes: (1) Decreased renal excretion (most common) - acute/chronic kidney disease, hypoaldosteronism (type 4 RTA, Addison disease), medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene, trimethoprim, NSAIDs, heparin, calcineurin inhibitors); (2) Transcellular shift - acidosis, insulin deficiency, beta-blockers, digoxin toxicity, tumor lysis syndrome, rhabdomyolysis, succinylcholine, hyperkalemic periodic paralysis; (3) Increased intake (rare alone) - supplements, salt substitutes, IV potassium, blood transfusions. Pseudohyperkalemia from hemolysis during phlebotomy, thrombocytosis, leukocytosis. Clinical manifestations primarily cardiac: peaked T waves, PR prolongation, QRS widening, sine wave pattern, ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Neuromuscular: weakness, flaccid paralysis, paresthesias.

Emergency treatment for K+ >6.0-6.5 mEq/L or ECG changes: (1) Cardiac membrane stabilization: calcium gluconate 1g IV (or calcium chloride 500mg via central line), repeat if ECG unchanged after 5-10 minutes; (2) Shift potassium intracellularly: regular insulin 10 units IV with 25g dextrose (onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6 hours); albuterol 10-20mg nebulized (adjunctive); sodium bicarbonate 50-100 mEq IV if acidemic; (3) Remove potassium: loop diuretics (if renal function adequate), potassium binders (sodium polystyrene sulfonate 15-60g PO, patiromer, sodium zirconium cyclosilicate), hemodialysis (definitive for severe cases or renal failure). Chronic management: dietary potassium restriction, review/adjust medications causing hyperkalemia, chronic potassium binders for CKD patients on RAAS inhibitors, treat underlying cause. Monitor for rebound hypokalemia after treatment.

Diagnostic approach: Confirm true hyperkalemia (repeat level, assess for hemolysis). Obtain immediate ECG for any K+ >6.0 or symptomatic patient. Assess renal function (BUN, creatinine), acid-base status (bicarbonate, ABG if severe), glucose. Evaluate medication list thoroughly. For persistent unexplained hyperkalemia: measure plasma renin activity and aldosterone to diagnose hypoaldosteronism (low aldosterone with high renin suggests primary adrenal insufficiency; low aldosterone with low renin suggests hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism/type 4 RTA). Calculate transtubular potassium gradient (TTKG) if uncertain about renal potassium handling: TTKG = (urine K × serum osmolality)/(serum K × urine osmolality); <7 suggests impaired renal excretion. Consider cortisol level if adrenal insufficiency suspected. Evaluate for rhabdomyolysis (CK), tumor lysis (LDH, uric acid, phosphate) if clinically indicated.
